quote: A society grows great when old men plant trees whose shade they know they shall never sit in.
through_line: Tuesday asks what grows slowly enough to matter. A new study on AI and creativity surfaces the question of what generative capacity actually means when the output is fluent but the process has no interior. Nieman Lab shows how new storytelling dimensions are opening up on the web; The Conversation grounds it in what children actually need to develop. And the thousand-year-old tree arrives as the week’s most patient image: some things are built not for this generation but for the one that hasn’t arrived yet.

articles.title: AI exposure affects human creativity, new research finds
articles.pitch: Researchers found that sustained exposure to AI-generated creative work affected how humans subsequently approached their own creative tasks — not by making them less creative, but by subtly shifting what they valued and pursued. The finding complicates the ‘AI as creative tool’ narrative and raises questions about the longer-term cognitive effects of living in an AI-saturated creative environment.

articles.title: Gen AI Website Traffic Share: February 2026
articles.pitch: The Digital Bloom’s February 2026 report tracks generative AI’s actual share of referral traffic across a sample of real websites — with numbers broken down by industry and device. For anyone building or advising on destination websites, this is the clearest current picture of how the traffic landscape is shifting. Recent, specific, and not hiding behind caveats.

articles.title: New dimensions for news storytelling
articles.pitch: A piece from Nieman Lab asking what new storytelling dimensions are becoming available to journalists and web editors — from scroll-driven narrative to audio-visual hybrids to interfaces that respond to reader behaviour. Not a roundup of trends but a genuine argument about where the expressive possibilities of the web are opening up.

articles.title: Kids need soft skills in the age of AI. But what does this mean for schools?
articles.pitch: Written for a general audience but grounded in educational research, this piece asks what ‘soft skills’ actually means when it becomes a policy recommendation — and whether schools know how to develop the ones that genuinely matter. The authors are honest about the difficulty of teaching adaptability and collaboration as if they were curriculum items.

articles.title: Embracing Our Nervous Systems with Plum Village Practice
articles.pitch: A dharma piece from Thich Nhat Hanh’s Plum Village journal on the nervous system as a practice space rather than just a physiological system. The framing is practical: how do we actually work with the body’s alarm systems during difficult moments? Precise and grounded, not motivational.

articles.title: How To Build A Thousand-Year-Old Tree
articles.pitch: A meditation on what it means to tend something that won’t reach its full form in your lifetime — using the ecology and culture of ancient trees as a lens on long-term thinking, care, and what we choose to pass on. The science and cultural history of how old trees actually grow carries the weight. One of those pieces that stays with you.
